title: Riding Global Tailwinds with EQT's Jean Eric Salata

segments.text: Speaker 1: Bloomberg Audio Studios, Podcasts, radio News.
Speaker 2: I'm Barry Ridhlts. You're listening to Masters in Business on
Speaker 2: Bloomberg Radio. My extra special guest today is Jean Eric Salata.
Speaker 2: He is chair of the QT Group, the largest alternative
Speaker 2: manager outside of the US. They manage over three hundred
Speaker 2: and sixteen billion dollars. Previously, he helped set up the
Speaker 2: Bearings Private Equity Asia Group and built it into one
Speaker 2: of Asia's premiere private equity platforms. With no further adoop,
Speaker 2: John Eric Salado, Welcome to Bloomberg.
Speaker 3: Thank you, Barry. It's great to be here. It's great
Speaker 3: to have you.
Speaker 2: I've been looking forward to this conversation for a while.
Speaker 2: Before we get to EQT, You've a really interesting background
Speaker 2: and I want to dive into that a little bit.
Speaker 2: You grow up in Chile, you go to the Wharton
Speaker 2: School at University of Penn to get a bachelor's in
Speaker 2: finance and economics. Was investing always the career plan?
Speaker 3: Well?
Speaker 4: I yes, investing was always the career plan. That's not
Speaker 4: how I ended up in Asia, but the idea and
Speaker 4: going to Wharton and becoming an investor was something I
Speaker 4: always wanted to do since I was a young boy.
Speaker 4: I remember reading a lot of biographies when I was
Speaker 4: a kid of business people and being very intrigued by that.
Speaker 4: I remember having my first paper delivery route when I
Speaker 4: was like ten or eleven years old and really enjoying
Speaker 4: the idea of making money, and then actually started investing
Speaker 4: that money as a young kid in the stock market
Speaker 4: as well, and kind of understanding how that worked. And
Speaker 4: then when I ended up at Wharton undergraduate and studying
Speaker 4: finance and management, I got very intrigued with global business
Speaker 4: outside the US. I come from an international background.
Speaker 3: My family.
Speaker 4: We grew up in South America. My grandparents actually came
Speaker 4: from Eastern Europe and were refugees that ended up in
Speaker 4: South America. Some generation a generation, we've been moving around
Speaker 4: quite a bit. I always felt like I had a
Speaker 4: quite a different perspective on life and the world than
Speaker 4: a lot of the people I was at school with,
Speaker 4: and so I was interested in pursuing that. And by
Speaker 4: as luck would have it or fate would have it,
Speaker 4: I ended up meeting my girlfriend at the time who's
Speaker 4: now my wife, who's from Hong Kong, and I ended
Speaker 4: up moving there.
Speaker 3: Right after I graduated.
Speaker 4: A year after I graduated from college and ended up
Speaker 4: really building my career in Asia as a result of that.
Speaker 2: And that was Hong Kong before the handover, so Chile
Speaker 2: Hong Kong. You started being as a consultants, end up
Speaker 2: everywhere from Sydney to Boston and then back to Hong Kong.
Speaker 2: Tell us a little bit about that global experience. How
Speaker 2: has that changed how you look at the world of investing.
Speaker 4: Yeah, I sort of have always felt a little bit
Speaker 4: like an outsider in the way I look at things.
Speaker 4: I never felt like I was exactly part of the
Speaker 4: community or the sort of the.
Speaker 3: Consensus view of things.
Speaker 4: I was always thinking about things a little bit more differently,
Speaker 4: I guess given the background. I was always comparing things
Speaker 4: when I was growing up in the US, I was
Speaker 4: always comparing things in the US to the way things
Speaker 4: were in Chile and saying, Oh, this is different or
Speaker 4: that's different. Then when I moved to Hong Kong, I
Speaker 4: had the same perspective. I was thinking, Wow, there's a
Speaker 4: lot that I see happening all my friends working on
Speaker 4: Wall Street or in private equity firms in the late
Speaker 4: eighties early nineties, that's not yet happening here in Hong Kong.
Speaker 3: It felt like there was a gap. It felt like
Speaker 3: there was a gap.
Speaker 4: There, and that always intrigued me and got me motivated
Speaker 4: and interested in thinking about starting something new that would
Speaker 4: sort of try to take advantage of it, or take
Speaker 4: advantage of that opportunity created by that gap. Of what
Speaker 4: eventually made me coming to Asia, that's already happening in
Speaker 4: the US, and that's sort of what led me to
Speaker 4: eventually leave insulting, get into private equity in the early nineties,
Speaker 4: which was really very early in Asian in in Asian
Speaker 4: context in the private equity industry, and from there sort
Speaker 4: of to start building the business.

Speaker 2: Investment there there? Did you help set up their pe
Speaker 2: arm or was that already up and running.
Speaker 4: No, that AIG was essentially an insurance business.
Speaker 3: AIG.
Speaker 4: Some of your listeners might recall Hank Greenberg, who's sort
Speaker 4: of a legend really kind of started He actually didn't
Speaker 4: start that business, but was really the founder that grew
Speaker 4: the business beyond the founder CV Star's initial starting of
Speaker 4: the business in Shanghai of ball places, and that became
Speaker 4: a large global insurance company. And in those days in Asia,
Speaker 4: there really wasn't a private equity industry, but there were
Speaker 4: insurance companies like AIG that had long dated liabilities and
Speaker 4: they needed to find long dated assets, and so you
Speaker 4: had stock market and fixed income and so on. But
Speaker 4: in the private markets there wasn't really a fund to
Speaker 4: invest in per se, so they started making their own
Speaker 4: investments off their balance sheet into companies to match their
Speaker 4: long dated liabilities. And so it was really working for
Speaker 4: AIG in their internal private equity group that got me
Speaker 4: started in the industry.

Speaker 2: a sentence you don't hear of that often.
Speaker 1: Yeah, it was.
Speaker 3: It was early days.
Speaker 4: It was interesting because you know, the whole region was
Speaker 4: really starting to boom. It was the golden period of globalization,
Speaker 4: you know, with the emergence of not just China, but
Speaker 4: Southeast Asia, Thailand, Indonesia, Taiwan, Korea, all these markets were
Speaker 4: starting to really develop and industrialize and there was a
Speaker 4: lot of requirement for capital for growth, and so we
Speaker 4: were really growth investors in those days, putting money to
Speaker 4: work behind companies and helping them to grow.

Speaker 2: executive president. You run finance for Shoeing Steel that's a

Speaker 4: do the private exit, So it was vain then shoeing,
Speaker 4: and then and then ai G. But the Shoeing experience, it's, uh,
Speaker 4: it's a part of my background that is a little
Speaker 4: bit different because it's really it's a family business that
Speaker 4: is an industrial company, very traditionally run. It's actually my
Speaker 4: wife's family business. So yeah, it's it's really uh, it
Speaker 4: was a very different experience.
Speaker 1: I went from I can imagine, Yes, I went from
Speaker 1: baning company, you know, sort of business schools down you know,
Speaker 1: everybody has similar backgrounds, very analytical, to the opposite end
Speaker 1: of the spectrum, which is it's a family business.
Speaker 4: Everybody who's in management is related to each other. And
Speaker 4: then uh, you know, you're making decisions based on sort
Speaker 4: of traditional ways of doing things.
Speaker 2: But this isn't a small little family dry claian Er.
Speaker 2: It's a big business, giant conglomerate. It was a.
Speaker 4: Sizable business and it was a good experience for me
Speaker 4: because it sort of helped me shape in the very
Speaker 4: formative years of my career, an appreciation for both.
Speaker 3: Sides of the spectrum.
Speaker 4: On the one hand, you have the need to be analytical, rigorous,
Speaker 4: understand global trends and sort of the way you look
Speaker 4: at things as a business school student. On the other hand,
Speaker 4: if you're going to do business in Asia, you have
Speaker 4: to be a little more entrepreneurial. You have to listen
Speaker 4: to your instinct. You have to be able to develop
Speaker 4: relationships with people, because ultimately, the decision makers in that
Speaker 4: part of the world, a lot of them have those
Speaker 4: sorts of backgrounds, and so you need to be able
Speaker 4: to understand how they think. And so that was a
Speaker 4: very valuable experience during my formative years. But I kind
Speaker 4: of came to the view that I didn't really want
Speaker 4: to spend the rest of my career in that sort
Speaker 4: of a setup, and so I applied to business school,
Speaker 4: and I got into business school. I got into Harvard
Speaker 4: Business School actually, and I was about to start at Harvard.
Speaker 4: I literally was there registered. I'm actually in the picture book,
Speaker 4: ready to go. And that's when I got the job
Speaker 4: offer to come back and work for this private equity
Speaker 4: division of AIG, which I decided ultimately that's really what
Speaker 4: I wanted to do rather go back to school again,
Speaker 4: having gone to undergraduate for a business degree already, and
Speaker 4: so I decided to defer my business school go back
Speaker 4: to work in Asia in private equity, and ultimately I
Speaker 4: actually never ended up really coming back to school.
Speaker 2: So after AIG, you helped launch a regional Asian private
Speaker 2: equity program for Bearing Private Equity Partners, a UK based bank. Right, yeah,
Speaker 2: I have the timeline, right, So I'm fascinated nineteen ninety seven.
Speaker 2: What was the investment landscape in Asia like in the nineties,
Speaker 2: Was that a very underappreciated set of opportunities or had
Speaker 2: people started to sniff out, hey, this area is going
Speaker 2: to be boom.
Speaker 4: It was a very very un volatile period actually, if
Speaker 4: you recall was going on at the time. So two
Speaker 4: things happened in nineteen ninety five. Nick Leeson, this is
Speaker 4: just around the time that I was joining Bearing Private Equity.
Speaker 4: Nick Leeson, who was a name that some of your
Speaker 4: listeners mare may recognize, others may not. He brought down
Speaker 4: this three hundred.
Speaker 3: Year old broke Bearings back.
Speaker 4: He broke the bank out of Singapore actually trading Japanese
Speaker 4: stock futures and kind of covering up his losses, which
Speaker 4: eventually brought the.
Speaker 3: Whole bank down.
Speaker 4: It was a you know, three hundred year old bank
Speaker 4: and one of the most prominent firms. So what ended
Speaker 4: up happening is that the Dutch firm Ing took over
Speaker 4: Bearings Famously for one pound and assumed all their liabilities
Speaker 4: and they took it over. This is around the time
Speaker 4: that I had joined, and you know, at the time,
Speaker 4: I remember thinking this is a very unsettling this is
Speaker 4: you know, I don't know what I'm going to do.
Speaker 3: I was very worried.
Speaker 4: I just decided to leave AIG and join this new
Speaker 4: company bearing private equity. In hindsight, sitting here today, I
Speaker 4: can say it's probably one of the best things that
Speaker 4: ever happened to me, was to be able to step
Speaker 4: into a situation that was going through a lot of change.
Speaker 4: And I think it is an important lesson in life
Speaker 4: actually that there are these times when you go through
Speaker 4: There's serendipity number one, so luck. There's also the fact
Speaker 4: that you're often thrust into situations you don't expect, and
Speaker 4: it kind of boils down to how you end up
Speaker 4: responding to them and looking for the best possible outcomes
Speaker 4: or the best way out of a situation can sometimes
Speaker 4: lead to huge opportunities, which is what happened here because
Speaker 4: that confusion of the takeover by IG of Bearings resulted
Speaker 4: in Bearings essentially figuring that they didn't need.
Speaker 3: To have some of these non core businesses.
Speaker 4: And so I approached the new Dutch owners and asked
Speaker 4: them if it was okay if we spun our business out.
Speaker 4: At the time, which we did, it was a very
Speaker 4: small business. It was a twenty five We had twenty
Speaker 4: five million dollars of assets under management, which even in
Speaker 4: those days was not a lot of mine, and we
Speaker 4: were really just getting started. And they agreed, and so
Speaker 4: we ended up establishing an independent, small private equity business
Speaker 4: called Bearing Private Equity Asian.
Speaker 3: So kept the name. We kept the name BPA.
Speaker 2: There was this tremendous transition from what was essentially a
Speaker 2: startup to what eventually became a pretty substantial institution. What
Speaker 2: was that like.
Speaker 4: Initially we were starting off and again it was nineteen
Speaker 4: ninety seven, ninety six, ninety seven, so if he recalled
Speaker 4: nineteen ninety seven was actually the Asian Financial crisis, as

Speaker 4: currency devaluations, starting with a ruble in Russia, but then
Speaker 4: sort of a contagion effect throughout.
Speaker 2: The ruble was worse the following year with long term
Speaker 2: capital management, if my memory is great. So the Asian
Speaker 2: contagion was the tie Bot crisis.
Speaker 4: And it was it was the Indonesian sort of high
Speaker 4: yield market as well that blew up. We're basically borrowing
Speaker 4: dollars because it was cheaper to do so, using that
Speaker 4: money to then invest in their businesses in Asia, thinking
Speaker 4: that they could make the spread and kind of capture that.
Speaker 2: As long as the currency stay stable, which.
Speaker 3: Is okay, but then it isn't till it isn't right.
Speaker 4: So that's what happened, and so that blew out and
Speaker 4: it caused a tremendous financial crisis across the whole region.
Speaker 4: And this is in the middle of when we were
Speaker 4: getting started. So I remember we're writing the first PPM,
Speaker 4: the first private placement memo to go raise capital, and
Speaker 4: the whole story was in ninety six was about growth
Speaker 4: in Asia, the growth story, and halfway through that writing
Speaker 4: the PPM, we had to basically change the PPM and
Speaker 4: change the strategy to become more of a distress strategy
Speaker 4: on how we're going to capitalize on the dislocation in
Speaker 4: Asia to invest in great companies that have bad balance sheets,
Speaker 4: which is sort of what we did with that first
Speaker 4: twenty five million that we started with, because what happened
Speaker 4: was that Ing gave us that seed capital to get
Speaker 4: going with, which was the twenty five million. They were
Speaker 4: supposed to give us three hundred but it ended up
Speaker 4: not coming through, so we started with twenty five.

Speaker 2: actual cash there's such as a multiple between the two.

Speaker 4: to be three of us that were coming across to

Speaker 3: You know, here I am.
Speaker 4: A young junior analyst and I get a chance to
Speaker 4: be potentially a partner in this startup, so I thought,
Speaker 4: I raised my hand. As we were about to get started,
Speaker 4: the two senior guys got a counter offer from Hank Greenberg,

Speaker 4: too important.
Speaker 3: We want you to stay.
Speaker 4: Here's all this money and equity and that, you know,
Speaker 4: to convince you to stay. But he didn't make me
Speaker 4: a counter offer. He just cut me loose. So those

Speaker 4: my own, and I went back to the IG folks
Speaker 4: and I said, here, I am, I'm ready to do this.
Speaker 4: They said, well, you're a little young and inexperience. It's

Speaker 4: less than ten percent, said I said. That's good enough
Speaker 4: for me. I said, I'll take that. That sounds good.
Speaker 4: So we started with twenty five and we did five
Speaker 4: deals of five million each, and it turned out that
Speaker 4: because of the cycle where we were, we were lucky
Speaker 4: to be able to buy in at good prices and
Speaker 4: we bought some interesting businesses.

Speaker 2: from an American perspective? Are those countries more similar than

Speaker 2: one is its own, independent, unique region.
Speaker 4: You're absolutely right about that, and that actually is the
Speaker 4: key I think to what we've been able to achieve
Speaker 4: over three decades, was that overcoming those barriers, because ultimately
Speaker 4: people think of Asia, they call it Asia, but it's
Speaker 4: really a first of all geographic, it's a huge, huge expansive.
Speaker 4: You know, from Tokyo to Sydney, it's like a twelve
Speaker 4: hour flight, you know, and from you know, from even
Speaker 4: from Hong Kong all the way to India, it's still
Speaker 4: a pretty long distance and culturally, you're talking about a
Speaker 4: very significant difference in the local culture, the language, the
Speaker 4: ways of doing business. So what we did initially was,
Speaker 4: you know, and we were actually criticized for this in
Speaker 4: the early days because in those days people just did single.
Speaker 3: Country funds for that very reason.
Speaker 4: You had a China fund, you had you know, you
Speaker 4: had a sort of Japan fund and Korea fund. And
Speaker 4: what we set out to do was to say, okay,
Speaker 4: we're going to create a regional investment program. People looked
Speaker 4: at me and said, what do you know about investing
Speaker 4: in Japan? Or like, what do you know about India?
Speaker 4: You're not even from Asia. And so what I early
Speaker 4: on appreciate it, and this has been an important lesson
Speaker 4: in my career is that actually being a good investor
Speaker 4: is very important for what we do in our industry.
Speaker 4: But if you want to build a company, which was
Speaker 4: always my ambition, if you want to build a business
Speaker 4: out of it, you need to actually build a team,
Speaker 4: not just be a good investor. Being a good investor
Speaker 4: is kind of prerequisite to be in our industry, but
Speaker 4: beyond that, it's really about building a team.
Speaker 3: And so I was lucky.
Speaker 4: Enough to meet and to and to bring on board
Speaker 4: some great partners early on, very diverse backgrounds. So you know,
Speaker 4: we have people even to this day and in those
Speaker 4: days from each of these markets. So you know, we
Speaker 4: had great partners from China, from Taiwan on our team
Speaker 4: that we hired early on. We had a very good
Speaker 4: team in India on the ground in Mumbai. We call
Speaker 4: it now local with locals, where you have local teams
Speaker 4: in each market. In two thousand and five, we opened
Speaker 4: up an office in Japan and we hired a great
Speaker 4: team in Japan of great people there. As we're building
Speaker 4: the team a you needed to have people from those
Speaker 4: markets that understood those markets. But the next question is
Speaker 4: how do you stitch it all together? You know, how
Speaker 4: do you create that common thread? And that comes down
Speaker 4: to culture and building a culture of like minded people.
Speaker 4: And so I started to really also gain a huge
Speaker 4: appreciation for the importance of culture in a business. And
Speaker 4: that's something by the way that EQT has I think,
Speaker 4: really excelled in globally. And one of the reasons I
Speaker 4: was ultimately attracted to EQT in combining our business with
Speaker 4: EQT four or five years ago was that Connie Johnson,
Speaker 4: the founder of EQT, early on with the Wallenberg's back
Speaker 4: backing realize that culture ultimately drives performance in an investment
Speaker 4: organization like ours. So built an organization with tremendous culture,
Speaker 4: and our culture was actually somewhat similar. So we were
Speaker 4: able to bring the two cultures together and the cultural
Speaker 4: fit ended up being what made that merger so successful.
Speaker 4: But going back to building the Asia business, building the
Speaker 4: team on the ground, building the common culture, and then
Speaker 4: it was sort of how do we institutionalize this instead
Speaker 4: of just doing deals here, doing deals there, how do
Speaker 4: we create a unified, systematic approach? And this is where
Speaker 4: my main days sort of came in of thinking, let's
Speaker 4: come up with some constructs about how we think about
Speaker 4: capital allocation, how we think about diversification, how do we
Speaker 4: think about macro, how do we think about sector trends,
Speaker 4: how do we think about our investment committee process? How
Speaker 4: do we drive due diligence systematic due diligence in every
Speaker 4: market so we have quality control in each market.
Speaker 3: It's not just random.
Speaker 4: Deal makers doing things the way that they want to
Speaker 4: do them on the ground, and so pulling all that together,
Speaker 4: which it took a lot of time. I'm making it.
Speaker 4: I'm shortening it here, but you know, there was a
Speaker 4: lot of ups and downs. There, a lot of mistakes,
Speaker 4: a lot of setbacks, but eventually we got there and
Speaker 4: we find our strategy over the years and we created
Speaker 4: something that's actually quite hard to replicate, which is this
Speaker 4: regional platform delivering consistent outcomes with a great team of
Speaker 4: consistent of people that have been with us a long
Speaker 4: time and that have a similar approach to underwriting and
Speaker 4: ultimately great performance. And so all that going from twenty
Speaker 4: five million where we ended up. By the time we
Speaker 4: did the deal with EQT, we had twenty five billion
Speaker 4: under management over the spans of what was twenty five
Speaker 4: years of billion.
Speaker 2: The business coming up, we continue our conversation with Jean
Speaker 2: Eric Salata, chairman of EQT Group, discussing the combination of
Speaker 2: BPA and EQT. I'm Barry Ridaults. You're listening to Masters
Speaker 2: in Business on Bloomberg Radio. I'm Barry Ridolts. You're listening
Speaker 2: to Masters in Business on Bloomberg Radio. My extra special
Speaker 2: guest this week is Jean Eric Salata. He is chair
Speaker 2: of EQT Group, one of the largest alternative managers outside
Speaker 2: of the US, they managed three hundred and sixteen billion dollars.
Speaker 2: So let's talk a little bit about how this all came.
Speaker 2: About twenty twenty two, you merge BPA with EQT. That
Speaker 2: was a seven billion dollar deal that followed about twenty
Speaker 2: five years of independence. What led to that decision to merge?
Speaker 2: What could EQT offer that BPA couldn't build on its own?
Speaker 1: Yeah?
Speaker 4: I think what I started to sense in about say
Speaker 4: twenty fifteen, was that the industry was changing. Our industry
Speaker 4: was changing global. You started to see global firms moving
Speaker 4: into Asia. You started to see some firms starting to
Speaker 4: go public. You started to see multi product firms developing
Speaker 4: beyond just a single product, single asset class scale. And
Speaker 4: I realized that although we were doing very well and
Speaker 4: we're very successful and growing, if we wanted to make
Speaker 4: this a multi generational business that's going to continue to thrive,
Speaker 4: we wanted to be part of this industry consolidation, put
Speaker 4: this trend towards scale, rather than to be sort of,
Speaker 4: you know, pushed aside by it. And that's when I
Speaker 4: started thinking, what are our options? I mean, one option
Speaker 4: was for us to try to expand beyond Asia and
Speaker 4: to develop our business outside of the region. That was
Speaker 4: going to be pretty difficult at this stage because the
Speaker 4: business is coming so large and entrenched globally. And then
Speaker 4: I started looking at ways of working with others, and
Speaker 4: that's when I met EQT really through their own IPO
Speaker 4: that they had done at the time gone public in
Speaker 4: twenty eighteen twenty nineteen, and I was curious about that,
Speaker 4: and I saw I went to speak to them about
Speaker 4: how they had done that, and we started talking, and
Speaker 4: you know, one thing led to another, and by the
Speaker 4: end of this conversation, it became evident to all of
Speaker 4: us sitting around the table that actually, there's something here
Speaker 4: that could be quite powerful if we were to come
Speaker 4: bring the businesses together.
Speaker 2: You mentioned earlier how important culture is to performance in
Speaker 2: an investment world. I would imagine that a Swedish firm
Speaker 2: like EQT and essentially a regional Asian firm like BPA,
Speaker 2: you would imagine those are very different cultures and there's
Speaker 2: going to be a challenge integrating the two of them.
Speaker 2: What was your experience like trying to get all the
Speaker 2: horses pouring in the same direction.
Speaker 4: Yeah, I think initially you could imagine that would be
Speaker 4: the case, but as it turns out.
Speaker 3: I think a few things here.
Speaker 4: First of all, started off as a Swedish firm, but
Speaker 4: it really by the time we met had already become
Speaker 4: a much more global business. So first Swedish, then European,
Speaker 4: expanding into Europe and then expanding into the US. Had
Speaker 4: some presence in Asia, not much. Secondly, EQT is backed
Speaker 4: by the Wallenberg family. The Wallenberg family is a sixth
Speaker 4: generation family from Sweden that has a history of really
Speaker 4: doing business globally. Investors in Ericsson, Electroluxe, SOB, many of
Speaker 4: the big Swedish companies Astrozeneca are backed by the Wallenberg family,
Speaker 4: and so they have a very global mindset, I would say,
Speaker 4: in the way they think about doing business.
Speaker 3: Globally and culturally.
Speaker 4: Then I think the other aspect here is there's a
Speaker 4: difference I think between a European firm like EQT and
Speaker 4: say American firms. The European firms already are thinking in
Speaker 4: terms of well, every country is different. You've got you know,
Speaker 4: the Nordics are different than Germany, which is different than France,
Speaker 4: which is different than Southern Europe. And so when they
Speaker 4: come to Asia they have I think a heightened sense
Speaker 4: of appreciation for the diff cultural differences within Asia, and
Speaker 4: you know, I think I think that to me was
Speaker 4: really important that they understand that within Asia, Japan is
Speaker 4: very different from India, and India is very different from China,
Speaker 4: and so I felt almost like there was a kindred
Speaker 4: spirit there and understanding that each country, each region, the
Speaker 4: cultures really matter. Then I would say that if you
Speaker 4: look at the histories of the firm, we're both about
Speaker 4: thirty years old at the time, we both had our
Speaker 4: ups and downs, we both kind of built the business
Speaker 4: from a founder of Connie and myself, and you know,
Speaker 4: I think there was a lot of common history, shared
Speaker 4: history there, and ultimately a point boiled down to the
Speaker 4: i'd say, the chemistry of the senior team, but then
Speaker 4: ultimately the culture throughout, and I felt very comfortable with it,
